segments.s756-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خسرو is the proper name (Khosrow). که introduces a relative clause 'who/whose'. چشم و دل is a binomial (literally 'eye and heart') meaning faculties of perception; روزگار here means 'the ways of time/fate'. Together the segment sets up a relative description of Khosrow: 'when Khosrow, who (in whose) eye and heart the fate (of things) ...'.
segments.s756-g10: نبیند is a negative present/subjunctive/indicative form of دیدن meaning 'does not see' or 'saw not' (here best read as 'did not perceive'). چنو (چنین) means 'such/like that', and نیز 'also/likewise'. یک شهریار means 'a single/any king' or more naturally 'any other king' — the clause completes the idea: 'he did not see any king like that.'
segments.s756-g11: چو = 'when'. شیروی را uses را as the definite/object marker (here marking the recipient Shiruy). شهریاری is the abstract noun 'sovereignty/royal authority', and دهد is the present tense 'gives' (third-person). The clause means 'when (he) gives/put Shiruy in possession of sovereignty.'
segments.s756-g12: همه شهر ایران is a genitive/grouping 'all the cities (or the whole land) of Iran'. به خواری دهد uses به plus an abstract noun خواری 'humiliation/abasement' and دهد again 'he gives/causes' — here دهد functions like 'brings (into)'. The whole segment says he causes all of Iran to fall into humiliation.
segments.s756-g13: چنو (چنین) = 'thus' or 'so'. رفت شد is a compound of رفت ('went') + شد ('became') used poetically to mark a change of state: 'went and became' = 'became'. تاجدار is a participial adjective 'crowned, crown-bearer', and اردشیر is the name Ardashir. Together: 'Thus Ardashir went and became crown-bearer.'
segments.s756-g14: بدو = 'to him' (directional/dative). شادمان here functions as 'rejoicing' or 'glad ones' (predicate describing reaction). جان برنا و پیر is a short nominal phrase 'the young in life (the young) and the old' — i.e. 'young and old'. The clause means 'to him (i.e. in his favor) rejoiced the young and the old.'
segments.s756-g15: مرا‌گر is an archaic/fused conditional 'مرا گر' = 'if to me / if it should come to me'. ز ایران رسد = 'should reach/arrive from Iran' (رسد in the sense 'reach/arrive'). هیچ بهر here functions as 'any (benefit)' — literal components mean 'any' (هیچ) and 'bother/for/advantage' (بهر). The whole segment sets a conditional: 'If any benefit should come to me from Iran...'.
segments.s756-g16: نخواهم is the first-person present negative 'I do not want/wish'. که introduces the subordinate clause. بر وی رسد uses بر + وی = 'befall/upon him', رسد here in the sense 'befall/come upon'. باد is the optative particle 'may' used with the verb to express the wished-for/undesired event; however negated by نخواهم it yields 'I do not wish that... may...'. شهر here means 'the realm/kingdom'. So: 'I do not wish that the kingdom should befall him.'
segments.s756-g73: فرستاده (noun) is the subject 'the messenger'. چُون شد is a temporal/sequence marker here — 'when/when he became/when he came' — with شد as the past tense of شدن ('become' / in context 'arrive/come to be near'). به نزدیک او is a prepositional phrase 'near him' (به + نزدیک + او) giving the goal/location; word order is verb + goal phrase, typical of Persian narrative style.
segments.s756-g74: چو here is a temporal/simile marker 'when/as'. خورشید شد is a copular clause 'became like the sun' (شد = became). جان تاریک اوی: جان 'soul/life' modified by تاریک 'dark'; اوی is an older/poetic possessive form meaning 'his'/'of him' (suffixal pronoun). The clause describes a change of state of his soul when the messenger approached.
segments.s756-g75: بیاورد is the past tense (3rd sing.) 'he brought'. زان is the poetic contraction of از آن 'from that', forming the source phrase زان بوم 'from that land/home'. چندان modifies سپاه and means 'so much/so many' — it quantifies the following noun (سپاه, 'army, host'). Word order is verb + source phrase + quantity + noun.
segments.s756-g76: که is the relative/linking particle 'so that/which', connecting to the previous clause. بر مور و بر پشه repeats the preposition بر ('over/against/on') for emphasis: 'over ants and over gnats' (small creatures used hyperbolically). بر بست راه is a verbal phrase where بست (past of بستن) 'closed' takes راه 'road' as object; 'the army closed/blocked the way.' The understood subject of this clause is سپاه from the previous segment.
segments.s756-g77: همی‌تاخت is an archaic/poetic imperfective past form (همی- prefix + past stem تاخت) meaning 'he kept charging' or 'he rushed'. چون باد is a simile 'like the wind'. تا + طیسفون means 'as far as Ctesiphon' (تا marking extent/destination). The clause describes rapid movement toward a place.
segments.s756-g78: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic ش indicating possession 'his army' (or 'the army of him/that one'). همه 'all' modifies دست 'hands', and شسته is the past participle 'washed' functioning adjectivally: 'hands washed (in...)'. به خون is an instrumental/ablative phrase 'in/with blood' describing the cause or stain. The whole NP conveys that the entire host's hands were blood-stained.
segments.s756-g79: ز لشکر is a poetic 'from the army' phrase (ز = از). نیارست here functions negatively/passively in older usage meaning 'did not/was not possible' (contextually: 'no one dared/none could'), while دم زد کسی is an inversion meaning 'did anyone utter a breath/sound' (literally 'someone struck a breath'). Together the clause means roughly 'from the army no one made a sound.'
segments.s756-g80: نبد is an archaic negative/existential verb 'there was not / did not exist'. خود here is a reflexive/emphatic 'itself'/'his own' and دران = در + آن 'in that'. شهر مردم بسی literally lists 'city — people — enough', so together it means 'in that city itself there were not many people' (بسی functioning as 'many/enough' in older usage). Word order (verb initial negative + topic + noun phrase) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s756-g33: بجویی is a 2nd-person present/optative form (verb-first, typical in epic advice) meaning “seek” or “may you seek.” بسی here is an intensifying quantifier meaning “many/much” and follows the verb; یار is the direct object “companions” and the adjectives برنا و پیر (“young and old”) follow the noun in coordination. The overall clause is an imperative/optative exhortation.
segments.s756-g34: جهان را is the definite object (‘the world’ + accusative/ topical marker را). بپردازی is 2nd-person present/optative of پرداختن used here in the sense “to remove/deprive” or “to strip (away)”; it takes جهان را as its object. از اردشیر → a prepositional phrase indicating from whom/what (i.e. ‘of Ardashir’). Word order is object–verb–prepositional phrase, normal for Persian poetry.
segments.s756-g35: ازان پس is a temporal sequence marker “after that / thereafter.” بیابی is 2nd-person present/optative (you will find/obtain). همه کامِ خویش is the object phrase “all your desires” (همه = all; کام = desire; خوی + ش = one’s own / your — the possessive is split here into خوی + ش). The verb governs the whole object.
segments.s756-g36: شوی is 2nd-person present/optative of شدن, “you will become” or “you will be.” ایمن و شاد are predicative adjectives “safe and joyful.” زارام is an archaic/poetic word meaning something like “miserable / distressing” (here treated as indicating the preceding misery being removed), and خوی + ش again form the possessive ‘your(=one’s own).’ The clause lists resulting states after the previous clause.
segments.s756-g37: گر introduces a conditional “if.” ایدون is a temporal/illustrative word (literally “come/appear” here functioning like “now/at this point”), and که introduces the subordinate content clause. این راز بیرون دهی is a verb phrase “you reveal/bring out this secret” where بیرون دهی (بیرون + دادن) means “to bring out / disclose.” The whole segment forms the protasis of the condition.
segments.s756-g38: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle (“indeed / then / ever” in epic diction). خنجرِ کینه را is the object phrase “the dagger of hatred” with را marking it as definite/accusative. خون دهی is a verb phrase where دهی (دادن) is used in the sense “to cause/inflict” (i.e. to cause bloodshed or make it bleed). Word order places the object before the verb, as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s756-g39: من is the 1st-person subject “I.” از روم is a prepositional phrase “from Rome/Byzantium.” چندان modifies سپاه and means “so great / such a large (army).” آورم is 1st-person present/future-like verb “I will bring” (classical Persian often uses present to indicate near-future or intent). The clause states the speaker’s promise/claim.
segments.s756-g40: که introduces the result clause “that …”. گیتی is the direct object “the world.” به چشمت is a dative/experiencer phrase “to/for your eye” (i.e. ‘before your eyes’). سیاه آورم uses آورم (“I bring / I cause”) with سیاه (“black”) to mean “I will make the world appear dark/black” — a poetic way of saying great destruction will befall the world in your sight. Word order is result–verb typical in subclauses.
segments.s756-g65: This is a nominal + verb clause where جفا پیشه (literally 'one who practices injustice') is a compound noun meaning 'the malefactor' or 'cruel man'. از پیش خانه is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of in front of the house' (از + پیش + خانه), and بجست is the past tense verb 'leapt/jumped out' placed after its subject phrase in typical Persian SOV order (here poetic word order).
segments.s756-g66: لب شاه is a genitive construction 'the king's lip' (لب + شاه). بگرفت is a past-tense perfect 'he seized/took hold of' that follows its object (typical in poetry). ناگه 'suddenly' is an adverb modifying the verb; به دست here means 'by/with the hand' (به used instrumentally) and دست is the instrument.
segments.s756-g67: همی‌داشت is an archaic/poetic imperfective construction meaning 'kept (on) holding' or 'was holding'. تا introduces a temporal/result clause 'until', followed by شد تباه اردشیر where شد is the past/become auxiliary 'became' and تباه is predicate adjective 'ruined' applied to the subject اردشیر (Ardashir). The clause therefore means he continued until Ardashir was ruined.
segments.s756-g68: همه کاخ is a noun phrase 'the whole palace' (همه as 'entire/whole' qualifying کاخ). شد is the copular/past 'became/was', and پر ز شمشیر و تیر is a predicative phrase 'full of swords and arrows' where پر 'full (of)' takes the poetic preposition ز = 'of/from' followed by the listed weapons.
segments.s756-g69: همه یار پیروز خسرو شدند is a simple nominal sentence: همه یار 'all the companions' (subject) + پیروز 'victorious' (predicate adjective) + خسرو (genitive/possessor) yields 'all of Khosrow's companions became victorious'. شدند is the past plural copula/verb 'became/were'.
segments.s756-g70: This short clause is conditional and elliptical: the particle اگر appears twice for emphasis ('if'), followed by نو جهانجوی 'a new/world‑seeking one'. گو is the stem meaning 'say' and بدند is a past-subjunctive/conditional form 'they would be' or 'they would (so) be'. Grammatically the clause omits an explicit object or complement and reads as a suspended conditional—literally 'if a new world‑seeker, if (he) were to say, they would be'—so the precise propositional content is left implicit in the poetry.
segments.s756-g71: This clause places the object هیونی ('a beast, savage creature') before the verb برافگند 'threw/ cast away'—a common poetic order. نزد گراز is a locative phrase 'near/toward the boar' (نزد = 'near/in the presence of'). The whole means 'he cast a beast toward the boar.'
segments.s756-g72: یکی نامه‌ای 'one/one of them, a certain letter' is an independent noun phrase; نیز 'also' adds that element, با آن 'with that' uses با as the instrumental/associative preposition, and دراز 'long' qualifies نامه. Poetically this clause is terse and can be read literally as 'also one long letter with that'—the exact referent of نامه (here 'letter' or a metaphorical object) is context-dependent and the phrase is intentionally elliptical.
segments.s756-g41: به ژرفی is an adverbial phrase meaning “deeply / with depth” (به here is adverbial, -ly sense). نگه‌دار functions as an imperative verb here (“keep / hold”), followed by the object گفتار من (“my speech / my words”). Word order is adverbial + imperative + object, a compact poetic command.
segments.s756-g42: مبادا at clause start is an optative/negative particle “may it not be / lest”; که introduces the subordinate clause. آیدت is the verb “it come / befall you” with the enclitic -ت “to you”; خوار is a predicative adjective “despised / lowly” describing کار من (“my deed / affair”), so the clause reads literally ‘lest my deed come to you despised.’
segments.s756-g43: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” پیروز خسرو is the subject (epic adjective before name: “victorious Khosrow”). چنان modifies نامه (“such a letter”), and دید is the past tense verb “saw / beheld.” Word order is typical: temporal marker + subject + object phrase + verb.
segments.s756-g44: همه پیش و پس is a compound adverbial meaning “all (in) before and after / in every respect.” رای خودکامه is the object phrase “the counsel/plan of the self-willed/autocratic (person),” where خودکامه modifies رای. دید repeats the past verb “saw.” The clause thus states what he perceived in full.
segments.s756-g45: This is a nominal-predicate line: دل روشن نامور is a stacked noun phrase (“the bright / clear, renowned heart”), شد is the inchoative past “became,” and تباه is the predicate adjective “ruined / destroyed.” Poetic ordering places multiple modifiers before the noun.
segments.s756-g46: که تا introduces a result/extent clause (“so that / to such an extent that”); چون here means “how”; کند is the present/subjunctive form “would do / could do”; بد qualifies the action as “evil / ill.” بد + ان (written here as ب دان) functions as به‌‌آن ‘to that’ (archaic contraction), so بدَان زادشاه = “to that king.” Overall the clause asks rhetorically how he could commit harm toward that king given the ruined state.
segments.s756-g47: ورا is the unstressed pronoun meaning “to him / to that one.” خواندی is a past action verb here best taken as “called / summoned / had (it) read to” (context allows ‘summoned’ or ‘caused to be read’), and هر زمان اردشیر gives a time phrase with the name Ardashir (“Ardashir at every time” / “you summoned Ardashir always”). The subject is implicit (poetic ellipsis).
segments.s756-g48: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “for / who.” گوینده is the head “the speaker,” followed by apposition مردی بد و یادگیر — “a bad man and a learner/memorizer.” Adjectives follow the noun in this descriptive listing.
segments.s756-g57: بفرمود تا introduces a commanded subordinate clause: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he said/ordered' and تا here marks the purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that they...'). برکشیدند is past, 3rd person plural ('they raised' / 'they lifted'); it is the main verb of the subordinate clause. رود here is the object, a musical instrument (not a river). Word order is verb-final for the subordinate clause (subject omitted).
segments.s756-g58: شد is the past/copular verb 'became' (resultative) and introduces the resulting state. ایوان پر از بانگ ... is the predicate: ایوان 'the iwan/portico' + پر از 'full of' + بانگ 'the sound/cry of' followed by two coordinated sources 'رود و سرود' ('rûd [the string instrument] and song'). The verb precedes the nominal predicate, giving 'the iwan became full of...'.
segments.s756-g59: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. نیمی شب is a genitive phrase 'half of the night' (نیمی = 'half'), with the adjective تیره 'dark' following and qualifying شب. اندرکشید is a past verb 'drew in / passed' (here describing the night drawing in), so the whole clause reads 'when half the dark night had drawn in/was come in.'
segments.s756-g60: سپهبد is the subject ('the spahbed'). می‌یک is an adverbial/quantifying phrase 'one draught/one sip'. در کشید is a compound verbal idea 'drew in / inhaled / took into (his chest)' with کشید the past verb at the end; together they mean 'took a draught'. The clause order is subject — object/measure — verbal phrase (typical older Persian).
segments.s756-g61: شده is a perfect/resultative participle 'had become' (or 'became') used with the adjective مست 'drunk' to form 'had become drunk'. یاران شاه اردشیر is a genitive chain 'the companions of King Ardashir' (یارانِ شاهِ اردشیر) with the head noun یاران modified by the following possessors.
segments.s756-g62: نماند is past 'remained / was left' and here negated by ایچ (an archaic form of هیچ 'nothing/no one'), so نماند ایچ = 'not a thing/person remained'. رامشگر و یادگیر are two coordinated nouns ('peacemaker/comforter and learner/memorizer') presented as the kinds of people who are lacking. Word order places the verb before the missing items for emphasis.
segments.s756-g63: بد اندیش is a compound/adjunct meaning 'ill-thinking' or 'bad-intentioned' (بد + اندیش). یاران is the plural subject 'the companions'. او را is the accusative object marker phrase 'him' (او = he; را marks the definite object). براند is the verb 'drove away' (agrees with the plural companions), placed at the end as typical in older Persian narrative.
segments.s756-g64: جز از is a prepositional construction 'except for'. What follows is a coordinated list 'شاه و پیروز خسرو' — note پیروز precedes the name as an epithet 'victorious Khosrow'. نماند at the end is the past verb 'remained' meaning 'no one remained except...'. The whole segment therefore means 'none remained except the king and victorious Khosrow.'
segments.s756-g17: نبودم is a past negative of شدن/بودن meaning 'I was not' (negative past = نبود + ـم). The order 'نبودم من آگه' places the verb before the pronoun and predicate for emphasis: 'I was not aware'. که introduces the following subordinate clause ('that ...'). پرویز شاه places the name then title (name + epithet).
segments.s756-g18: به here has an instrumental/causal sense 'by' ('by the speech of ...'). گفتار is the noun 'speech/words'. آن refers back to those previously mentioned people. بدتنان (an older plural form) means 'the bad/ill‑disposed ones' (people with ill will). شد followed by تباه is a compound verbal idea 'became ruined' (شد = 'became'). تباه = 'ruined/corrupt'.
segments.s756-g19: بیایم is an optative/subjunctive form ('may I come' / 'let me come') used here as a proposal or determination. کنون = 'now'. با governs an accompaniment 'with'. سپاهی گران is a noun + adjective: سپاهی 'army/force' and گران 'heavy/valuable' in the sense 'mighty/weighty' (i.e. formidable troops).
segments.s756-g20: ز ... و ز ... is the poetic repeated preposition 'from ... and from ...'. روم = 'Rome/Byzantium' and ایران = 'Iran'. گزیده is a participial/adjectival form 'chosen/elite'. سران is the plural of 'سر' used as 'leaders/chiefs' and follows the adjective in the noun phrase 'گزیده سران' = 'chosen chiefs'.
segments.s756-g21: ببینیم is first‑person plural subjunctive/hortative 'let us see' or 'we will see'. تا introduces the content clause here with a sense close to 'to/so that' and together with کیست forms the question 'who is'. کیست is the copula 'is' (کی + ست = 'who is'), where ست is an older/poetic form of است. این کدخدای = 'this village‑head / petty chief' (demonstrative + title).
segments.s756-g22: که begins the relative/explanatory clause 'who ...'. باشد is subjunctive/potential 'may be' or 'would be' (here marking indirectness or doubt). پسندش is پسند + enclitic -ش 'his liking/approval' (object/clitic referring back to someone). The sequence ب + دین is orthographically the preposition+demonstrative 'بدین' ('by/with this'); here the provided tokenization splits it, so ب = 'by/with' while دین does not carry its ordinary meaning in this context (see note). گونه 'manner/kind' and رای 'opinion/counsel' form 'بدین گونه رای' = 'with such counsel/opinion'.
segments.s756-g23: چنان 'thus/so' gives manner. برکنم is a first‑person singular subjunctive/imperative 'I will pluck up/remove' (برکندن = to uproot/remove); here it's a resolutive, 'I will uproot'. بیخ 'root' is the direct object; او is the third‑person pronoun 'his' or 'him' (here 'his root'). را is the definite object marker attached after the pronoun. ز بن = 'from the base/foundation' (z = 'from', بن = 'root/base'). Word order places the verb before its object for emphasis.
segments.s756-g24: کزان is a compound/relational prepositional form 'from which/whence' linking the consequence clause. پس here means 'afterwards/thereafter' or 'so then'. نراند is the negative/form 'ن' + راند (rāndan 'to drive') meaning 'will not drive/drive away' (the negative prefix integrated). ز شاهی سخن = 'the speech/talk of kings' (ز = 'of/from', شاهی = 'royal/kingship' used adjectivally, سخن = 'speech'). The whole clause states the intended result: thereafter royal talk will not be able to drive (him).
segments.s756-g25: نوندی is a proper-name-like form (the -ی ending marks the noun), functioning as the subject. برافگند is a past-tense verb (برافکندن) meaning 'sent forth/threw (away/onwards)'; پویان is the plural present-participial noun 'those who go / travelers / the active ones' and is the direct object of برافگند. به راه is a prepositional phrase 'onto the road' (به + noun). Word order here is Subject – Verb – Object – prepositional complement, typical of poetic narrative.
segments.s756-g26: به نزدیک is a locative phrase 'near / close to'; پیران ایران is a genitive construction 'the elders of Iran' (پیران = elders, ایران = Iran). سپاه (army) is the logical subject, placed after the locative phrase: the copula is omitted (typical in past narrative), so the clause reads 'the army was near the elders of Iran.'
segments.s756-g27: دگرگونه is an adverb/adjective meaning 'differently/another way'; آهنگ here means 'intention/aim' and is the internal object; بدکامه is an adjective modifying آهنگ ('ill‑willed, malicious, ill‑fated'). کرد is the past tense of کردن, used as a light verb to mean 'made / took' (i.e. 'took/formed an ill‑willed intention'). The phrase is therefore 'he made/took a different, ill‑willed intention.'
segments.s756-g28: به + name (به پیروز خسرو) is dative 'to Pīrūz Khosrow' (پیروز functioning as epithet/title). یکی نامه is 'one / a certain letter' (numeral/determiner + noun). Here کرد (again 'to do/make') combines with نامه — a common Persian expression نامه کردن — meaning 'to write / send a letter.' The verb is past tense, so the clause means 'he wrote/sent a letter to Pīrūz Khosrow.'
segments.s756-g29: که here introduces/explains the clause ('that' or 'since'); شد is the past-tense of شدن 'became'; تیره is a predicate adjective meaning 'dark/ominous/ill‑fated'; این تخت ساسانیان is the noun phrase 'this throne of the Sasanians' (تخت = throne; ساسانیان = the Sasanian dynasty). Word order places the predicate before the subject, a permissible poetic inversion: 'that this Sasanian throne became ominous.'
segments.s756-g30: جهانجوی is a noun meaning 'world‑seeker' or 'ambitious person' (the agent expected to act). باید is the modal 'must / it is necessary' and is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که. بندد is the present-subjunctive form (بند + -د) of بستن/بستن-derived sense 'to bind / to close / to interpose', used here with implied object; میان is the adverbial 'between.' The whole phrase expresses obligation: 'a world-seeker must interpose between (them).'
segments.s756-g31: توانی is the second-person singular present/subjunctive form 'can you / are you able' used interrogatively. مگر here works as a rhetorical particle ('perhaps / then / can it be that') adding emphasis to the question. چاره‌ای is an indefinite object 'some remedy/means' (چاره with the indefinite suffix -ای). ساختن is the infinitive 'to make / to find' and with the preceding object yields 'can you find/make a remedy?' The verb (infinitive) appears at the end as typical.
segments.s756-g32: ز is the poetic form of از 'from/of' and introduces the source/material; هرگونه means 'every kind / any sort'; اندیشه is 'thought / thinking'; انداختن is the infinitive 'to throw / to cast' (here used metaphorically 'to cast away / rid oneself of'). Together this infinitival phrase expresses the action sought: 'to cast away every kind of thought.'
segments.s756-g49: برآسای is a compound/adverbial idea (from برآسودن) meaning 'at ease/soothed' or 'by the arrangement'; it precedes the noun دستور ('order/arrangement'). بودی is the past copula 'was' and ورا is the dative/indirect-object clitic 'to him/for him' placed after the verb, so the phrase reads literally 'was at ease by the order to him' (i.e. 'he was comforted by the arrangement for him').
segments.s756-g50: همان نیز ('that same also') places emphasis on the same person/item and the enclitic نیز adds 'also'. گنجور is a predicate noun 'treasury / treasure‑house' describing that person; بودی is again the past copula 'was'; ورا is the dative 'to him'. Word order is noun phrase + copula + indirect object (archaic placement of ورا after the verb).
segments.s756-g51: بیامد is the past verb 'came' used impersonally/third‑person; شبی is an adverbial 'one night' (placed after the verb, a common classical order). تیره‑گون is a compound adjective 'dark‑colored' (تیره + گون) modifying بار ('load/burden/parcel'); یافت is the past 'he found', so the clause reads '(one) dark‑hued night came; (he) found a dark load.'
segments.s756-g52: می‌روشن here functions adjectivally/participially as 'bright/illuminating' (descriptive, not a literal progressive verb), joined by و to the adjective چرب 'flattering/rich', both modifying گفتار 'speech'. یافت is the past verb 'he found' placed at the end; together: 'he found (a) bright and flattering speech.'
segments.s756-g53: نشسته is a participle 'seated' used predicatively; به ایوان خویش is a prepositional phrase 'at/on his iwan/verandah' where خویش is the possessive ('his own') formed from خوی + the clitic ش; اردشیر, the proper name, appears after this phrase (classical Persian allows the subject to follow the predicate).
segments.s756-g54: تنی چند is an idiom meaning 'a few persons / several bodies' (i.e. 'a few men'). با او 'with him' indicates companionship. ز before برنا و پیر is the classical preposition 'from/of' used to list classes ('of the young and the old'); برنا = young, پیر = old. The phrase as a whole lists Ardashir's companions by age.
segments.s756-g55: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. پیروز 'victorious' modifies the proper name خسرو. بیامد is the past 'came/arrived'. برش here is a contracted form (likely بر + ش) meaning 'to him / before him' (a clitic attached); this compressed form is common in classical usage and places the direction/recipient after the verb.
segments.s756-g56: تو گفتی 'you said' — گفتی is the 2nd‑person past of گفتن (here introducing reported speech). ز گردون 'from the sky/heavens' gives origin, برآمد is the past 'arose/rose up', and سرش 'his head' (سر + clitic ش) is the subject of that verbal phrase. Word order places the reporting verb (تو گفتی) before the quoted/result clause.
segments.s756-g1: پس is a temporal particle 'then'. آگاهی (a noun) is the subject 'news/word' and آمد is the past tense verb 'came' — noun + past verb is a normal simple clause. به نزد گراز is a prepositional phrase of destination/presence ('to/into the presence of the boar'), with نزد used poetically for 'in the presence of/near'.
segments.s756-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'). ز (poetic form) here means 'from / because of' (ز + او → 'because of him' / 'on his account'); بود is the past of 'to be' (here 'was') with the subject خسرو placed after it (poetic/inverted order). به + adjective phrase گرم و گداز expresses a state 'in/with heat and burning ardor' — گداز is a poetic noun meaning ardor/anguish rather than literal 'melting'.
segments.s756-g3: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent' (transitive). گوینده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a speaker/messenger' (the -ای ending gives an indefinite/one-of sense) and را is the definite object marker marking گوینده‌ای as the direct object. ز روم is a prepositional phrase 'from Rum' (Rum = Byzantium/Byzantine lands).
segments.s756-g4: که introduces the content of the message. در خاک = 'in the land/soil' (خاک used as 'land/realm'). شد is the past of 'to become' (change of state). تاج is the grammatical subject 'crown', شیروی is an adjectival/epithet 'lion‑like / brave' modifying تاج, and شوم here is an adjectival result 'ill‑omened / accursed' (so 'شد ... شوم' = 'became ... ill‑omened'). Note the poetic inversion (verb before subject).
segments.s756-g5: که again introduces a clause (here part of a curse). جانش = جان + suffix ـش 'his soul/his life' (possessive clitic). به دوزخ 'into/to Hell' (دوزخ = hell). گرفتار is a predicate/adjective 'caught/trapped', and باد at the end is the optative particle 'may (it be)' — a fixed formula for a curse in older Persian.
segments.s756-g6: سر دخمه او is a genitive grouping 'the head/top of his tomb' (سر = head/top, دخمه = sepulchre/tomb, او = he/his). نگون is an adjective 'downcast/ill‑fated/overturned'. سار here appears in a formulaic curse and is lexically 'wanderer' or 'starling' in the provided senses but functions idiomatically; the final باد is again the optative 'may'. The sequence expresses a wish that his tomb's top be overturned/ill‑fated.
segments.s756-g7: که introduces the clause; دانست is past 'he knew' / 'he supposed'. هرگز 'never' negates the verb — the construction دانست هرگز که ... = 'he never thought/assumed that ...'. The second که begins the subordinate clause; سرو بلند is the noun phrase 'the tall cypress' (noun + adjective).
segments.s756-g8: به باغ 'in the garden' (به + place noun). از گیا 'from the plants/vegetation' (source/agent of harm). گزند یافت is a compound idea in Persian meaning 'to suffer harm' (گزند یافتن = 'to receive harm'); in the poem the elements are split and ordered poetically: یافت (main lexical verb 'to find/receive') with خواهد (future auxiliary 'will') and گزند ('harm') together yield 'will receive harm'. So the clause says that the tall cypress will receive harm from the vegetation in the garden.

sentences.s756-s5: When Khosrow — whose eye and heart do not perceive the ways of fate — saw no other king like that.
sentences.s756-s6: When he gave sovereignty to the brave Shiruy, he brought all the cities of Iran into humiliation.
sentences.s756-s7: Thus Ardashir went and became crowned; both young and old rejoiced for him.
sentences.s756-s8: If any benefit should reach me from Iran, I would not wish that the realm fall upon him.
sentences.s756-s37: When the messenger came near him, his dark soul became like the sun.
sentences.s756-s38: He brought from that land such a host of soldiers that they blocked the road against ants and gnats.
sentences.s756-s39: He charged like the wind as far as Ctesiphon; his whole host had their hands washed in blood.
sentences.s756-s40: No one from the army uttered a sound; in that city itself there were scarcely any people.
sentences.s756-s17: Seek many companions, young and old, and strip the world of Ardashir.
sentences.s756-s18: After that you will attain all your desires; you will be safe and joyful, freed from your misery.
sentences.s756-s19: If now you disclose this secret, you will indeed give blood to the dagger of hatred.
sentences.s756-s20: I will bring so large an army from Rome that I will blacken the world before your eyes.
sentences.s756-s33: The malefactor leapt out from in front of the house; he suddenly seized the king by the lip with his hand.
sentences.s756-s34: He kept holding (on) until Ardashir was ruined; the whole palace became full of swords and arrows.
sentences.s756-s35: All of Khosrow's companions became victorious; if (a) new world-seeker — if they were to say — they would be (so).
sentences.s756-s36: He hurled a beast near the boar; also there was one long letter with it.
sentences.s756-s21: Keep my words in depth; may it not be that my deed becomes despised by you.
sentences.s756-s22: When the victorious Khosrow saw such a letter, he saw all the autocratic counsel, both before and after.
sentences.s756-s23: The bright, famed heart became ruined, so (it was) that how could he do ill to that king?
sentences.s756-s24: You summoned him — Ardashir — at every time, for the speaker was a bad man and a learner.
sentences.s756-s29: He ordered them to raise (it), and the iwan became full of the sound of the rûd and song.
sentences.s756-s30: When half the dark night had drawn in, the spahbed took a draught.
sentences.s756-s31: Ardashir the king's companions had become drunk; not a single peacemaker or learner remained.
sentences.s756-s32: His ill-intentioned companions drove him away; none remained except the king and the victorious Khosrow.
sentences.s756-s9: I was not aware that King Parviz had been ruined by the words of those ill‑disposed.
sentences.s756-s10: Let me now come with a heavy army, chosen chiefs from Rome and from Iran.
sentences.s756-s11: Let us see who this kadhkhudā (village‑chief) is, who could have his approval for such counsel.
sentences.s756-s12: I will thus uproot his root from the foundation, so that thereafter the talk of kings will not drive (him).
sentences.s756-s13: Navandi sent the travelers forth onto the road; the army drew near the elders of Iran.
sentences.s756-s14: He took a different, ill‑willed course of action and wrote a letter to Pīrūz Khosrow.
sentences.s756-s15: That this Sasanian throne became ominous — a world‑seeker must, it is necessary, interpose between them.
sentences.s756-s16: Can you, then, find some way to cast away every sort of thought?
sentences.s756-s25: He was a comfort to him by the arrangement, and that same man was also a treasury to him.
sentences.s756-s26: One dark‑hued night came; he found a dark load, and he found bright, flattering speech.
sentences.s756-s27: Ardashir sat on his own verandah, a few men (both) young and old with him.
sentences.s756-s28: When the victorious Khosrow arrived to him, you said that his head arose from the heavens.
sentences.s756-s1: Then word reached the boar that Khosrow was consumed with heat and burning ardor.
sentences.s756-s2: He sent a messenger from Rum, saying that in the land the lion‑like crown had become ill‑omened.
sentences.s756-s3: May his soul be seized in Hell; may the vault of his tomb be overturned.
sentences.s756-s4: He never supposed that the tall cypress in the garden would ever suffer harm from the vegetation.
